speaker: interviewee
text: We are now in 1938. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1938, life started as beautiful as always. In Austria, we had the Social Democratic Party at that time, and I still remember Schuschnigg was the Chancellor. And-

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was--

speaker: interviewee
text: --14. I just was 14 in 1938. And we just went-- everything went fine. And we knew that things went on in Germany, you know, that Adolf Hitler was in Germany. But basically, we didn't pay too much attention, as strange as it seems. I remember we had some children that came to our schools and-- who came from Germany and settled in Austria, Jewish families. And they entered our schools and just lived onward, you know, in Vienna. And we never paid much attention to what really-- we knew what was going on, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew that there was Nazis. And that we also knew that a lot of people where became Nazis, but-- in Vienna-- but it was underground. And what the Nazi Party really did was they gave people a schilling a week to belong to the Nazi Party underground, and that is how they got them to join. Because people were very poor during that period of time. It was-- the Depression had been in 1929, in 1930, and people were really poor. And so anything that gave them some additional income, they were able to get.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that played a major part when Hitler marched into Austria, that they had the underground party of the National Socialist Party. And because during the Social Democratic or Christian Socialist Party, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] life just went on very, you know, it was a republic. And we just lived very quietly, and everybody did what they needed to. So then along came [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] March the 13th. It was about 7 o'clock in the evening. We were sitting around the dinner table. And all of a sudden, over the radio came the announcement-- the German Reich just marched into Austria. The Anschluss, which means acquiring, has been completed. Inquiring?

speaker: interviewee
text: Acquiring.

speaker: interviewer
text: Acquiring, yeah. The Anschluss means to-- [? CREW: ?] Include.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to include. The inclusion of Austria into the German Reich has been completed. And we really didn't understand what-- what they were saying. And about 10 minutes later, we looked out the window, and policemen were walking with swastikas on their arms on the streets. Now, those were the same policemen that were in Austrian uniforms in the afternoon at 3 o'clock. There they were. We saw people running around with swastika flags. And about two hours later, we heard the droning of-- of airplanes. And at that time, you know, you had double-deckers, single-deckers. You had tiny little planes compared to what we have today. But the motors were so loud, and they flew so low that it absolutely was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Deafening.

speaker: interviewee
text: --ear-shattering.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was very intimidating, the sound. And also motorcycles, they came-- like 30 or 40 motorcycles were running up and down the streets, on the main streets. And that went on for about 24 hours, the constant noise of-- of-- I don't think they had tanks at the time. But it was vehicles of-- war vehicles of some kind. May have been motorcycles with little carts next to them. But it was very overpowering, and it was frightening, extremely frightening.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- at first, we didn't realize what-- what is going on here. And my mother said-- and she says, let's pack up and leave. And my father said to her, are you out of your mind? Everything we own we have here. I have investments here. I have businesses. I have apartment houses. We can't just leave like this. She says, let's go. He says, no, we're not leaving.

speaker: interviewee
text: And within two days, my father's business, three men walked in in brown shirts and says, from this moment on, we're running your businesses. We will let you stay here. But when it comes to what we take in in monies, we will let you know how much you can keep and what we are taking. That was the very first sign. The second one was, within three or four days big signs in grocery stores, [GERMAN], means we don't serve dogs and Jews, and the same was on park benches.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you actually go out into the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, yeah. And after two days, we went out. And the one thing that I would like to make-- that really was one of the major factors that made us survive-- we were blond and blue-eyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole family.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my sister, myself. My father was-- had brown hair. But that really was one of the very major factors. And as-- after-- as I said, after the two days and three days passed, and the pomp and circumstance that they carried on with marching bands, the pictures that you may have seen on newsreels with thousands of people marching, these were the facts. I was there. I saw it. I went on the streets. I was not that afraid. Being a 13, 14-year-old child, you don't have the intense fear that an adult will fear. And so it was very unusual for me, you know, especially where you led a life like we did, so sheltered. And here was all this going on, and the bands were playing. And it was mass hypnosis that they exerted against or to the people. That's when Hitler spoke for two and three and four hours at a time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, absolutely, absolutely. And it was-- it was like a hypnosis. He had the people hypnotized.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you feel when you heard that, when you listened to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- because of my what parents explained to us at home, we looked at him as he is-- this is our enemy. What is he doing here? How can he do that? And you have to realize that seen from the eyes of a 13 or 14-year-old child is totally different than if you're an adult or if you're grown up, and you look at things in a different manner. You don't have the fear as much. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. I'm interested how you felt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's-- as time went on, the fear became more and more intense, which I will explain to you how it became that way. And in school, after-- in April we were thrown out of school. When we came to school after the Anschluss and everybody had to stand up in class and raise the arm and say Heil Hitler, and of course, we Jewish children did not do this. It was-- I mean, that is when they said, OK, all Jews, home. No more learning. This is it. You don't go to this school anymore. It was difficult for us to understand. I mean, the friends that we had in school, they looked down at us. They said, you're a Jew, go. We don't want you. The pain, it was very painful for a child. But I played with you yesterday. Well, my mother and father don't want you around. You stay away from us. And it was very difficult.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents did explain to us of truly what-- what the basis of everything is. And my father says, you remember when I said to you they can take everything away from us? This is what I was talking about. And within three days, they confiscated all his belongings.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had cash in the house, which was locked up, quite a bit, but unbeknownst to us children. We were not made aware of it. And so my father, I don't know how, what, but he was able to hide money. And-- because at that time, you don't wrote checks, and you don't do this. Everything was dealt in cash. And there where-- we had certain areas, and we had things where we could hide inside our home, which we did. I remember we had compartments in our dining room set that had-- where you pushed a button. And I learned that later on. I didn't know that we had that. And mama had a safe in her bedroom somewhere that I never even knew she had. So we had cash that we hid. And-- but these were the tangible assets. The intangible ones, they completely confiscated everything that my father had. So luckily, he was able to, prior to that, to turn some of the assets, or the intangibles, into liquid cash. So we could plan. Because it became a very, very important factor during that year of survival in-- in Vienna. Cash was everything. And again, as I said, we did not realize it. My parents never discussed money with us. This was something you didn't speak about. And we just knew that if we needed something, it was here. [SIGHS] And the difficult thing that we started to realize was people who were your friends yesterday spit on you. They called you dirty Jew. And I always said, well, I'm not dirty. I'm clean. I-- I wash. I bathe every day. I'm not dirty. You're nothing but a dirty Jew, and you should die like a dog. And very shortly after, about a month after, I was on the street, and I saw SS men walking with doberman pinscher dogs, and they had two on each-- each side. They were-- they were holding the reins of the dogs and these black-- big, black dogs. And I saw a truck at the end of the street. And there was a plank that was lined up on the back end of the truck, and I saw they were herding people into the truck. And at that moment, all of a sudden, I said, oh, my god, these are Jewish people that I knew from the neighborhood. And one man couldn't run as fast as they wanted. So this SS guy let go of the dogs, and he sicced them on the people there. And as a child, when I saw how those dogs attacked a human being, this never went out of my mind. To this day, I will never enter a home if there's a doberman dog in that house. I will never have-- if I see a doberman on the street, I will run on the other side. It is something that you can never, ever forget, that vision of those dogs attacking the people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Excuse me. This is-- this is bad for me at this moment, because I see the picture in front of me again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they kill them, the dogs? They killed the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: All I saw was ripping, ripping. And I just went-- I ran. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then the next thing that was so devastating to us is when I saw them-- and again, if anybody looked Jewish, they gathered you up. And I remember ladies, friends of my mother's that she used to have tea with, we saw them being rounded up in the streets, pushed down on their hands and knees, given lye. They threw a pail of lye at them and said, Jew, scrub the streets. And they made the people lay on the ground scrubbing the streets with lye. And then they took the buckets, and they threw it over the people. And there is no way you can express-- this is when my fear started to develop, which was not in the beginning, and how they tried to rob you of your dignity. Because, you know, you can-- they can take anything away from me. They can never rob me of my dignity. I will always have my dignity. Whether you're poor has no bearing on it. You still have your dignity. And they made as out to be lesser than-- lesser than a dog. And this was-- came more and more and more to the forefront. And one of the most devastating things that we-- that I experienced was I had a little girlfriend, and her name was Suzi Borash. She was a beautiful little girl. And we went to school together, and we played together. She was born in Hungary, and her parents had come to Vienna. Her father was a very nice businessman, and her mother was a lovely woman. She looked like Zsa Zsa Gabor, her mother. And Suzi called me on the telephone and says, let's go and go for a walk. And for some reason, my mother, at that time, said no, you're not going out of the house. You're staying home. I said, but, mama, I want to go with Suzi. She said no. I won't-- I don't want you going out today. And I didn't hear from Suzi for about two days. And all of a sudden, when I got in touch there, I was told-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] let me backtrack. About a half a mile away from where we lived, there was an SS headquarters, a building where the SS had their headquarters and the SR. And I was told that she was passing by the headquarters there by herself. They pulled her into the building, and a day later they threw her out, totally raped, molested, body, this beautiful child, and threw it on the street. And her parents found it and took it home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, thank you. For the grace of god, there go I. And these are the things that, all of a sudden, you grow up very quickly as a child. And you say to yourself, what is happening? What's happening to people, to humanity? How can anybody do that to a little girl who has never hurt anybody's hair on their body? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a very, very traumatic experience, and I've never gotten over it. And as you notice, not even today can I talk about it without being emotional about it. And-- but we had to survive. We had to keep on going. And they started to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father tried to hide wherever he could. And finally we had to hide him up in the mountains by bribing certain people to hide him in some--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because he was a man?

speaker: interviewee
text: And a Jewish man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because of the circumcision?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, yes. That's-- and then also people were extremely jealous of people that were successful. And you had people that were a nobody yesterday, and today, all of sudden, they're a-- a big shot and a [GERMAN], and they-- they-- they couldn't even count two and two together. But they belonged to the party, and they became big shots. And we found out that we have-- we knew that we had relatives in America. And we were able to contact them, and to see if we could get visas to come to the United States. Well, it wasn't very easy. So we tried to find out if there are other areas that we could run to. South America, we had relatives. It was practically impossible there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't want to stay in Europe, even though relatives at that time-- because Poland wasn't taken over yet-- they said, why don't you come to Poland? Come on, we'll, you know, we'll-- you'll live with us. And for some reason, my parents felt, no, we have to go further than that. There was an instinct there in some way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so in order to even undertake the idea that you want to leave the country, you had to have all kinds of certificates, all kinds of-- don't forget. They took everything we had already, that-- and they still wanted more money. You got to pay us for this. You got to pay us for that. Unless you paid and kept paying and paying, you couldn't even get a certificate to tell you that, yes, you can leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had to stand on line at night, overnight, stand in line to those offices in order to even get this. So sometimes we stood 12, 14 hours on line to just to get a rubber stamp on a piece of paper. And there were times when you had maybe 300, 400, 500 Jews standing in line in front of these offices. And they would take a truck and just go right into the people and drive into them and just harm and hurt them. And I know it's very difficult for people in this generation, in our generation now, the young people, to understand or to realize what went on. But the fear that we experienced, which later on I tried to explain to my son, how can I explain to you what fear is if you've never experienced fear? How can I explain to you what pain is unless you have experienced pain? And words cannot-- and you don't know what hunger is unless you have really been hungry in your life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What did you eat in those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to-- again, through bribing, bribery, because they wouldn't sell to Jews. So you had to find people that you could bribe to buy food for you. And there were only a handful of people that would help you because they didn't want to help a Jew. There was a handful who would do it just to get the money, and there was maybe one or two who would do it out of the goodness of their heart. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so we just kept on going. In the meantime, the summer was passing. You know, it was during the summer. And my father found out that there is a school that the-- it was called Israeli [NON-ENGLISH] Kultusgemeinden. They had school, a little school, classes, that you can learn a trade quickly. So if you go to a country, at least your hands can support you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father enrolled me in that school, and I learned how to do leather goods, and I was taught how to do pocketbooks. And so it was like an instant trade that they taught you, and sewing, which was, of course, that I always enjoyed. So that is what I-- and my sister went to study. It was a course about nannies, children's nurses. So we thought that if she-- if we go anywhere, at least we can have-- excuse me-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she can work as a nanny and so on. So that is what we did. And I did go and took that course, and I believe it lasted four months or six months, something like that. And so I was busy going to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the meantime, it was getting more difficult for my father. And as I said, we finally hid him up in the hills. And my mother told us, if anybody comes here to ask where papa is, you tell them you don't know. My parents had a fight, and they split. I have no idea where my father is. He left us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they fight about?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't. That was a lie. I was supposed--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see. That was the story, yeah. Sorry, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was supposed to tell them that this is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --what--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --what they did. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when-- in the meantime, we were able, again, with bribery and so on, we were able to at least apply for visas. My mother was born in Russia. We were born in Vienna. But my father was born in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what-- what we found out is our quotas were open. The Russian and the Austrian quotas were open, but not my father. And my father was two and a half years until his quota would be open. So he got-- took a number. We always had to take numbers on quotas. And we all got numbers. And my parents decided that they would split. We received the affidavits from our relatives here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And an affidavit, if some of the listeners don't know what it is, is that your relatives in this country sign for you that you will never take a penny from the government in-- at that time there was no welfare-- but in any way, shape or form will never, ever go to the government for any help whatsoever. And they signed for that. And that's the only way you could come to the United States, a little different than it is today. And so we did have the affidavits they signed for us, and we split. And what we had to do was split our affidavits so that my parents could leave the country.
